## QUASI-NEWTON POLICY GRADIENT ALGORITHMS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Policy gradient algorithms have been widely applied to reinforcement learning (RL) problems in recent years. Regularization with various entropy functions is often used to encourage exploration and improve stability. In this paper, we propose a quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm with entropy regularization. In the case of Shannon entropy, the resulting algorithm reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropy functions, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms. We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. Using synthetic and industrial-scale examples, we demonstrate that the proposed quasi-Newton method typically converges in single-digit iterations, often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Throughout the paper, the state space _S_ and the action space _A_ are assumed to be finite. A policy _π_ is a randomized rule of action-selection where _πs[a]_[denotes the probability of choosing action] _[ a]_[ at state] _[ s]_[.][For a given policy] _[ π]_[, the value function] _vπ_ defined as

This problem can be solved by, for example, the policy gradient (PG) method. However, the vanilla PG method converges quite slowly. In Agarwal et al. PG method with entropy regularization and some of its variants, the convergence rate can be improved to _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ), i.e., linear convergence (Mei et al., 2020), which can still be slow since _c_ is in general close to 0. It is also demonstrated in numerical examples that these algorithms with linear convergence rate can suffer from slow convergence. For example, in the example in Zhan et al. (2021), thousands of iterations are needed for the algorithm to converge, even though the model is relatively small and sparse. Therefore, there is a clear need for designing new methods with faster convergence and one idea is to take the geometry of the problem into consideration. The Newton method, for example, preconditions the gradient with the Hessian matrix and obtains second-order local convergence. Since the exact Hessian matrix is usually too computationally expensive to obtain, the quasi-Newton type methods, which use structurally simpler approximations of the Hessian instead, are more widely used in generic optimization problems, and are known to enjoy superlinear convergence (Rodomanov & Nesterov, 2021a;b).

## 1.1 CONTRIBUTIONS

In this paper, we investigate the quasi-Newton approach for solving (5). The main contributions of this paper are the following.

- First, we present a unified quasi-Newton type method for the policy optimization problem. The main observation is to decompose the Hessian as a sum of a diagonal part and a remainder, where at the optimal solution the remainder part vanishes so that the Hessian becomes diagonal. This inspires us to use only the diagonal part in the quasi-Newton method. As a result, the proposed method not only leverage the second order information but also enjoys low computational cost due to the diagonal structure of the preconditioner used. When the negative Shannon entropy is used, this method reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropic regularizations, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms.

- Second, we analyze the convergence property of the proposed quasi-Newton algorithms and demonstrate local quadratic convergence both theoretically and numerically. By leveraging the quasi-Newton framework (Dennis & Mor´e, 1974), we provide a simple and straightforward proof for quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. In the numerical tests, we verify that the proposed method leads to fast quadratic convergence even under small regularization and large discount rate (close to 1). Even for industrial-size problems with hundreds of thousands states, the quasi-Newton method converges in single-digit iterations and within a few minutes on a regular laptop.

## 1.2 BACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK

A major workhorse behind the recent success in reinforcement learning (RL) is the large family of policy gradient (PG) methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 1999), for example, the natural policy gradient (NPG) method (Kakade, 2001), the actor-critic method (Konda & Tsitsiklis, 2000), the asynchronous advantage actor-critic (A3C) method (Mnih et al., 2016), the deterministic policy gradient (DPG) method (Silver et al., 2014), the trust region policy optimization (TRPO) (Schulman et al., 2015a), the generalized advantage estimation (GAE) (Schulman et al., 2015b), and proximal policy optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), to mention but a few. The NPG method is known to be drastically faster than the original PG method, intuitively because in the NPG method the policy gradient is preconditioned by the Fisher information (an approximation of the Hessian of the KL-divergence) matrix and fits the problem geometry better. This idea is extended in TRPO and PPO where the problem geometry is taken into consideration via trust region constraints (in terms of KL-divergence) and a clipping function of the relative ratio of policies in the objective function, respectively. These implicit ways (in the sense that they do not adjust the gradient by an explicit preconditioner) of adjusting the policy gradient is in essence similar to the mirror descent (MD) method (Nemirovskij & Yudin, 1983) in generic optimization problems.

This similarity in addressing the inherent geometry of the problem is noticed by a line of recent work including Neu et al. (2017); Geist et al. (2019); Shani et al. (2020); Tomar et al. (2020); Lan (2021), and the analysis techniques in MD methods have been adapted to the PG setting. The connection was first built explicitly in Neu et al. (2017). The authors consider a linear program formulation where the objective function is the average reward and the domain is the set of stationary state-action

distributions, in which case the TRPO method can be viewed as an approximate mirror descent method and the A3C method as an MD method for the dual-averaging (Nesterov, 2009) objective. As a complement, Geist et al. (2019) considers an actor-critic type method where the policy is updated via either a regularized greedy step or an MD step and the value function is updated by a regularized Bellman operator, which also includes TRPO as a special case, and error propagation analysis is provided. In Shani et al. (2020), an adaptive scaling that naturally arises in the policy gradient is applied to the proximity term of the MD formulation, and sublinear convergence result is proved with a properly decreasing learning rate. In Tomar et al. (2020), the application to the nontabular setting is enabled by parameterizing the policy and applying MD to the policy parameters, and the corresponding sublinear convergence result is presented.

Regularization, a strategy that considers the modified objective function with an additional penalty term on the policy, is another crucial component in the development of PG type methods. Intuitively, regularization is able to encourage exploration in the policy iteration process and thus avoid local minima. It is also suggested (Ahmed et al., 2019) that regularization makes the optimization landscape smoother and thus enables possibly faster convergence. Linear convergence result is then established for regularized PG and NPG methods (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020). In these relatively earlier works (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020), the regularization usually takes the form of (negative) entropy or relative entropy. In the more recent work Lan (2021) and Zhan et al. (2021) that follow the MD type methods, the regularization is extended to general convex functions with the resulting Bregman divergences different from the KL-divergence and linear convergence is guaranteed as well.

However, most of these algorithms are of either sublinear or linear convergence except the entropy regularized NPG with full step length (which is a special case of the quasi-Newton method we propose), and even the linear convergence rate _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ) can be slow since _c_ can be close to zero. This motivates us to invent the quasi-Newton policy gradient method to be introduced in Section 2.

## 4 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT I

We first test the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 on the model in Zhan et al. (2021). For the sake of completeness, we include the model description here. The MDP considered has a state space _S_ of size 200 and an action space _A_ of size 50. For each state _t_ and action _a_ , a subset _St[a]_[of] _[ S]_ is uniformly randomly chosen such that _|St[a][|]_[ = 20][, and] _[ P][ a] tt[′]_[= 1] _[/]_[20][ for any] _[ t][′][∈][S] t[a]_[.][The reward is] given by _rs[a]_[=] _[ U] s[ a][U][s]_[, where] _[ U] s[ a]_[and] _[ U][s]_[are independently uniformly chosen on][ [0] _[,]_[ 1]][.][The discount] rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

In the numerical experiment, we implement Algorithm 1 with the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence with _α_ = _−_ 3. We set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as _ϵ_ = 10 _[−]_[12] and the learning rate _η_ as 1. Figure 1(a) demonstrates that, for these four tests, the quasi-Newton algorithm converges in 7, 7, 7, and 6 iterations, respectively. In comparison, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in Zhan et al. (2021) to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion. As also shown in Figure 1(a), many more iterations are needed for GPMD and PMD to reach the same precision: GPMD converges in 14822 iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after 3 _×_ 10[5] iterations.

In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥F |_ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where _π[∗]_ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm. A green reference line with slope log 2 through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥|_ shall be parallel to the reference line. The convergence curves approach the reference lines in the end (and are even steeper than the reference lines in the begining), demonstrating clearly a quadratic convergence for all regularizations used here.

## 4.2 EXPERIMENT II

Next, we apply the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 to an MDP model constructed from the search logs of an online shopping store, with two different ranking strategies. Each issued query is represented as a state in the MDP. In response to a query, the search can be done by choosing one of the two ranking strategies (actions) to return a ranked list of products shown to the customer. Based on the shown products, the customer can refine or update the query, thus entering a new state. The reward at each state-action pair is a weighted sum of the clicks and purchases resulting from the action. Based on the data collected from two separate 5-week periods for both ranking strategies, we construct an MDP with 135k states, and a very sparse transition tensor _P_ with only 0 _._ 01% nonzero entries. The discount rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient is _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this paper, we present a fast quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm that is capable of solving large MDP problems with large ( _≈_ 1) discount rate and small ( _≈_ 0) regularizations. The proposed method includes the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm as a special case, and naturally extends to other regularizers such as the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence.

Theoretically, we show local quadratic convergence of the proposed quasi-Newton method with a relatively simple proof. This quadratic convergence is confirmed numerically on both medium and large sparse models. In contrast with mirror descent type methods (e.g. Zhan et al. (2021)) that take up to tens of thousands iterations even with manually tuned learning rate, the proposed quasiNewton algorithms typically converge in less than 10 iterations, despite the large discount rate (close to 1) and small regularization coefficient ( _τ ≈_ 0).

For future work, we plan to adapt the technique used here to other gradient based algorithms for solving the MDP problems. Other types of _f_ -divergence can also be included. Another direction is to consider continuous MDP problems by leveraging function approximation, effective spatial discretization, or model reduction.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling approach to policy optimization by framing the Natural Policy Gradient (NPG) and its potential extensions as quasi-Newton methods. The primary strength of the work lies in its theoretical ambition: providing a unified framework that derives NPG from Shannon entropy and generates new algorithms for alternative entropy functions, all while promising local quadratic convergence—a significant leap over the linear rates common in existing LITERATURE. The observation that the Hessian becomes diagonal at the optimal solution is a clever insight that justifies the computational efficiency of the preconditioner. However, the paper’s quality is currently hindered by significant formatting issues; the text extraction reveals pervasive LaTeX/encoding errors (e.g., “_e[⊤] vπ_”, “[] _a[π] s[a]_”), which make the mathematical arguments difficult to parse. Furthermore, while the industrial-scale results are promising, the "high-level" nature of the introduction leaves a gap regarding the specific "other entropy functions" and their practical utility. To be successful at a venue like ICLR, the authors must ensure that the transition from the tabular setting (implied by the finite state/action space assumption) to the "industrial-scale" examples is clearly bridged, as second-order methods often struggle with the high dimensionality of neural function approximators. If the quadratic convergence holds in practical, non-tabular settings, this contributes a major result to the field.

comments.title: Loose definition of 'quasi-Newton' vs. 'approximate Newton' methods
comments.quote: In this paper, we use a more general definition of the term “quasi-Newton method”. Unlike classical quasi-Newton methods where the approximate Hessian matrix is constructed using firstorder information, the proposed quasi-Newton method uses second-order information
comments.explanation: In standard optimization literature (e.g., Nocedal & Wright), 'quasi-Newton' specifically refers to methods that approximate the Hessian using only first-order gradients to satisfy the secant equation (like BFGS). The authors' method—simply using the diagonal of the actual Hessian—is more accurately termed an 'approximate Newton' or 'diagonalized Newton' method. Using the label 'quasi-Newton' for a method that explicitly calculates second-order spectral information may confuse readers familiar with the traditional distinction where quasi-Newton methods avoid second-order calculations entirely.

Critically, the limit as $\alpha \to -1$ for $\varphi(x)$ involves a division by zero in the $4/(1-\alpha^2)$ coefficient. While the authors likely intend to recover the reverse-KL, the specific functional form provided is singular at that point. In most literature and in the 'Key Equations' section of this paper, the Advantage or Q-value related term is $(r + \gamma P v - v)$. Here, $f(\pi)_{sa}$ is defined with a leading negative sign: $-(r_s[a] + \gamma \sum P v_t - (v^\pi)_s)$. However, the update in Eq 17 uses $\dots = - 	ext{diag}(w) (f + 
abla \Phi \dots)$. If $f$ already contains a negative sign, the double negation might change the descent direction to an ascent one or vice versa, depending on how $
abla \Phi$ (the gradient of the regularizer) is signed. In the summary, $h^\pi$ is 'negative Shannon entropy', so $
abla \Phi$ would be the gradient of the penalty. Usually, in policy gradient, we maximize reward - penalty. A careful check of the signs in (17) compared to (20) is needed to ensure the 'accelerated mirror descent' connection holds. If $f$ is the negative advantage, then $-f$ in (20) would be the advantage, which is correct for ascent.

comments.title: Possible OCR Error in Shannon Update Line 8 of Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: Looking at the Shannon update defined in the 'Key Equations' section ($\pi_s[a] \leftarrow \frac{\pi_s[a] \exp(\eta Q_s^\pi[a] / \tau)}{\sum_{a'} \pi_s[a'] \exp(\eta Q_s^\pi[a'] / \tau)}$), the text in Step 8 of Algorithm 1 appears to be a severely corrupted OCR misread. While I can infer the authors' intent to implement the established Shannon update, the actual output in the algorithm block is unintelligible (e.g., '[p(] [η][(] [η][r]'). A reader relying solely on this pseudocode to reproduce the Shannon-entropy case would be unable to do so without referencing the introductory summary or external literature.

comments.title: Incorrect Equation Reference for Search Log Example
comments.quote: When calculating _vπ_ by _vπ_ = ( _I − γPπ_ ) _[−]_[1] ( _rπ − τhπ_ ), we apply the iterative solver Bi-CGSTAB (Van der Vorst, 1992)...
comments.explanation: The text states they are calculating $v^\pi$ using $(I - \gamma P^\pi)^{-1}(r^\pi - \tau h^\pi)$. However, referring to the 'Notation & Definitions' in the summary, $r^\pi$ is already defined as $(r^\pi)_s = \sum_a \pi_s[a] r_s[a] - \tau (h^\pi)_s$. Therefore, the formula in the text effectively subtracts the regularization term twice. It should either be $(I - \gamma P^\pi)^{-1} r^\pi$ or $(I - \gamma P^\pi)^{-1} (\bar{r}^\pi - \tau h^\pi)$ where $\bar{r}$ is the unregularized reward. This is a technical inconsistency in the notation.

comments.title: Misleading Figure Captions in Experiment II
comments.quote: Figure 2: Figures for the synthetic medium scale MDP.
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 2 describes it as 'Figures for the synthetic medium scale MDP.' However, Section 4.2 explicitly describes this experiment as 'an MDP model constructed from the search logs of an online shopping store' with 135k states and industrial ranking strategies. This contradicts the 'synthetic' label used in the Figure 2 caption (which is correctly applied to Figure 1). While a minor editorial slip, it creates confusion about which dataset the quadratic convergence evidence in Figure 2 actually supports.

comments.title: Confusion between Local and Global Convergence Proofs
comments.quote: The convergence curves approach the reference lines in the end (and are even steeper than the reference lines in the begining), demonstrating clearly a quadratic convergence for all regularizations used here.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to prove 'local quadratic convergence' (Newton-type) in Theorem 5. However, the experiments initialize with a 'uniform policy,' which is far from the optimal policy in most sparse MDPs. The authors interpret the 'steeper' initial slope as evidence of fast convergence, but quadratic convergence is specifically an asymptotic property ($||e_{k+1}|| \le C ||e_k||^2$) near the optimum. Claiming the experiments 'verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3' when the algorithm starts far from the optimizer might overstate the alignment between the theory (local) and the empirical results (which seem to show global efficiency).

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a quasi-Newton approach to policy gradient methods with entropy regularization, positioning itself as a significant advancement in optimization for reinforcement learning. The core idea—leveraging a diagonal approximation of the Hessian derived from a decomposition that exploits the structure of the policy optimization problem—is insightful and well-motivated. The authors effectively contextualize their method within the broader landscape of policy gradient algorithms, drawing meaningful connections to natural policy gradient, mirror descent, and other geometry-aware methods. The theoretical contribution, particularly the claim of quadratic convergence via a simple and general proof within the quasi-Newton framework, is promising and potentially impactful. Moreover, the empirical results suggesting convergence in single-digit iterations—even on large-scale industrial problems—are striking and, if reproducible, would represent a substantial practical improvement. However, the paper raises several high-level concerns that currently undermine its credibility and clarity. First, the transition from the general formulation in Section 1 to the specific quasi-Newton derivation lacks sufficient detail in the early sections; the key decomposition of the Hessian and the exact form of the proposed preconditioner are only vaguely described, making it difficult to assess the novelty and correctness of the method without further exposition. Second, the abstract and introduction heavily emphasize "quadratic convergence" and dramatic speedups, but the convergence analysis is only briefly sketched and appears to be local in nature—this creates a risk of overstatement, especially given that global convergence guarantees and robustness under varying regularization strengths are critical in RL and not adequately discussed. Third, while the method allegedly recovers NPG with Shannon entropy, the precise equivalence is not clearly established, and the extension to other entropies lacks illustrative examples or ablation studies to confirm its generalizability. Finally, the paper is cut off mid-sentence during the discussion of related work, which raises concerns about completeness and readiness for review. Overall, the paper presents a compelling idea with strong potential, but the clarity of the technical presentation, completeness of the theoretical argument, and robustness of the empirical claims require significant strengthening before the contributions can be fully evaluated.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of φ for α-divergence undermines theoretical results
comments.explanation: The paper defines φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) for α < 1, but this is inconsistent with standard α-divergence definitions. For α = 0, this yields a constant function (φ(x) = 1), not the Hellinger divergence as claimed. As α → −1, φ(x) → 1, not the reverse-KL generator (e.g., −log x). The correct generator should be proportional to (1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) / (1−α²) for convergence to classical divergences. The current form causes φ′(x) → 0 as α → −1, preventing recovery of reverse-KL. This flaw invalidates the theoretical derivation in Theorem 2 and the α-divergence updates in Algorithm 1, including experimental results relying on α = −3. While the exponent σ = 2/(α−1) for the inverse mapping is consistent with this φ, the root issue is the incorrect f-divergence generator, which undermines the method's foundation for α-divergences.

comments.title: Correctability of α → −1 limit depends on flawed φ definition
comments.quote: When α = −1 (the reverse-KL divergence), the corresponding update scheme is πs[a][←]  1 π−s[a] η[−][η][|][A][|][(] [r] s[a][−][[(] [I][−][γP][ a]_[)] [v][π]_[]] [s]_[)] [/τ][+] [ c][s]  −1
comments.explanation: The claim that α → −1 recovers reverse-KL divergence hinges on the φ function. However, with φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}), the expression does not converge to a reverse-KL generator. Instead, φ(x) → 1 (constant), which lacks the required log structure. Although φ′(x) ∝ x^{(α−1)/2} suggests that for α = −1, θ ∝ 1/π (correct for reverse-KL), the normalization factor 4α² → 4 corrupts the limit. This indicates a likely coefficient error (e.g., 4α² should be 4/(1−α²)). As such, the reverse-KL correspondence is mathematically invalid under the current definition, though it might be recoverable if the φ expression is corrected.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 contains structurally incorrect KL divergence update
comments.explanation: The KL update in Algorithm 1 has a critical flaw: it uses 'exp(η(η r_s[a] + (γP^a v_π)_s)/τ)', which incorrectly scales the reward by η^2 and adds the value term instead of properly computing the advantage. The correct form should be π_new ∝ π^{1-η} exp(η (r_s[a] - (I - γP^a)v_π)_s / τ). The current expression mishandles signs and scaling. While OCR artifacts affect readability, the structural error (double η, addition of (γP^a v_π)_s without subtracting v_s or combining into the advantage) suggests a deeper issue than formatting. This undermines the KL baseline, a key comparison in experiments. Even if c_s absorbs v_s, the η^2 r_s[a] term is unjustified and would cause incorrect updates.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in Theorem 2 update rule breaks geometry for general α
comments.explanation: The update rule (14) applies a linear interpolation in θ-space: θ ← (1−η)θ + η(...). However, in Theorem 2, θ_s[a] is defined as φ′(|A|π_s[a]), which for α-divergence is nonlinear (e.g., π_s[a]^{(α−1)/2}). A linear update in this space corresponds to Euclidean geometry, not the Bregman geometry induced by φ. Correct mirror descent requires updating in the primal space or via the inverse mirror map. The given update is valid only for KL divergence (φ′(x) = log x), where θ-space is log-space and linear interpolation approximates natural gradient flow. For other α, this breaks the connection to quasi-Newton or mirror descent, invalidating the theory for general α-divergences.

comments.title: Underspecified normalization for general entropies and missing derivation of x_a
comments.quote: The determination of cs can be done in a similar way as in Ying (2020) based on the following lemma. The proof of this lemma can be found in Appendix C.
comments.explanation: While Lemma 3 and Algorithm 1's bisection method solve for c_s in the α-divergence case, the procedure for arbitrary convex φ is not specified. The bisection relies on the form (c_s + x_a)^σ, which assumes a power-law inverse mirror map, only valid for specific φ. For general φ, the normalization becomes a nonlinear system with no guarantee of univariate monotonicity. Furthermore, the derivation of x_a in Algorithm 1 (line 13) is not linked to the theoretical update in Theorem 2. The corrupted expression suggests a mismatch in powers and coefficients, and the correct construction of x_a from θ_{old} and the gradient term is not provided. This gap makes the method non-reproducible for entropies beyond the α-family.

comments.title: Incorrect sign and term handling in Shannon entropy update (Theorem 1)
comments.explanation: The update rule for θ_s[a] includes an unexplained −τ term inside the numerator: (r_s[a] - τ - [(I-γP^a)v_π]_s). This suggests the entropy regularizer contributes -τ to the reward, but standard natural gradient derivations do not yield such a constant subtraction. The gradient of the regularized objective includes -τ ∂h/∂π = -τ(1 + log π_s[a]), which when transformed via Fisher metric and parameterization θ = log π, results in an update dependent on advantage minus τ, but without subtracting τ from the immediate reward r_s[a]. The inclusion of -r_s[a] - τ is unjustified and likely a sign or constant error that misaligns the update from correct natural policy gradient behavior.

comments.title: Misleading presentation of quadratic convergence without qualifying assumptions
comments.quote: We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'quadratic convergence' without noting that Theorem 5 requires η=1, the iterate to be sufficiently close to θ^*, Lipschitz gradients, and local convergence. Presenting this as a general characteristic risks misleading readers into expecting rapid global convergence. While the theorem is correct, the high-level description overstates the practical implications, especially when accompanied by claims of 'single-digit iterations' without specifying favorable initialization or idealized conditions.

comments.title: Ambiguous matrix remainder notation in Hessian approximation
comments.quote: Hr is a remainder that vanishes at π = π[∗], i.e., Hr = O(∥π − π[∗]∥)
comments.explanation: The claim that Hr = O(∥π − π*∥) for a matrix-valued Hr is ambiguous without specifying the matrix norm. The intended meaning is likely ∥Hr∥ = O(∥π − π*∥) in some operator norm, but this is not stated. Given that the convergence proof depends on this asymptotic behavior, the lack of norm specification weakens the rigor and could mislead about the approximation's strength.

comments.title: Unjustified use of wπ as preconditioner in mirror descent analogy
comments.quote: where diag( _wπ_ ( _k_ )) is the diagonal matrix with the diagonal equal to _wπ_ ( _k_ ) := ( _I − γPπ[⊤]_[(] _[k]_[)][)] _[−]_[1] _[e]_[,]
comments.explanation: The paper identifies diag(wπ) as a preconditioner in a mirror descent scheme, with wπ = (I - γPπ)^{-T} e. However, wπ arises from the dual formulation of the policy gradient, not from the Bregman geometry of the regularizer. In standard mirror descent, the preconditioner comes from ∇^2 Φ(π), not from a value-like dual variable. The appearance of wπ here is an algebraic coincidence rather than a geometric justification. While the update form may be preserved, the semantic claim of 'adaptive learning rates β_s = 1/(wπ)_s' lacks derivation and risks misattributing the convergence mechanism.

comments.title: Ambiguity in normalizing constant notation (wππs[a]) in Hessian approximation
comments.quote: where H[˜] is a diagonal matrix given by H[˜] ( sa ) , ( tb ) = −τδ{ ( sa ) , ( tb ) } ( wππs[a] ) s
comments.explanation: The term (wππs[a])_s is not properly defined. It likely means wπ[s] ⋅ πs[a], the product of the s-th component of wπ and the action probability. However, the juxtaposed notation 'wππs[a]' is confusing and could be misread as a matrix-vector product or a vector indexing error. Given that this defines the diagonal preconditioner, clarity is essential. The lack of standard notation or explicit definition increases the risk of misinterpretation during implementation.

comments.title: Incorrect sign in regularized reward definition
comments.quote: With hπ, the original reward rπ is replaced with the regularized reward rπ = rπ − τhπ
comments.explanation: At first glance, this equation appears circular: it defines rπ in terms of itself. On closer inspection, the intended meaning is that the regularized reward is rπ − τhπ, where rπ on the right-hand side is the original (unregularized) expected reward under policy π. However, the reuse of rπ on both sides of the equation creates confusion. The authors likely intend to overload rπ to denote the regularized reward after this point, as suggested by 'we overload the notation v for the regularized value function' later. But unlike v, which is explicitly redefined via (4), rπ is not clearly redefined. In standard RL notation, rπ usually denotes the expected reward under π, and the regularized reward would be rπ − τhπ. Thus, the issue is not the concept but the presentation: defining rπ = rπ − τhπ is syntactically incorrect, akin to writing x = x − y. A better formulation would be to define a new term, say r̃π = rπ − τhπ, or to clearly state that rπ now denotes the regularized reward. The context partially resolves this, as the value function (4) uses rπ − τhπ, so rπ in that context must be the original reward. But in this sentence, the notation is genuinely ambiguous and could mislead a reader unfamiliar with such conventions.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in policy parameterization in Theorem 2 update rule
comments.explanation: This update for θs[a] appears identical to Equation (11) in Theorem 1, which was derived under the assumption θs[a] = log πs[a], corresponding to Shannon entropy. However, in Theorem 2, θs[a] is redefined as φ′(|A|πs[a]), where φ is a nonlinear function specific to the α-divergence. Applying a linear convex combination update (like in (14)) in the θ-space only corresponds to a valid quasi-Newton or mirror descent step if θ is the conjugate (gradient) mapping associated with the Bregman divergence. While such updates are common in mirror descent (where θ = ∇Φ(π)), the form in (14) — a simple linear interpolation in θ — is equivalent to mirror descent only with a constant step size and specific geometry. More critically, the update rule (14) does not reflect the actual nonlinear dependence of θ on π introduced by φ′. For example, if θ = φ′(|A|πs[a]), then a proper mirror descent step should involve updating π in the primal space via the mirror map, or equivalently, updating θ via a non-Euclidean step. However, here the update is linear in θ, suggesting a Euclidean projection, which mismatches the geometry induced by the α-divergence. The context does clarify in Remark 3 that θs[a] = φ′(πs[a]/µa), implying it's meant to be the mirror map variable. But the update rule (14) fails to account for the nonlinearity of φ′, making it incorrect for all cases except KL-divergence (where φ′(x) = log x + const, so θ ≈ log π). For other α, φ′ is not logarithmic, and a linear update in θ-space does not preserve the required geometry. This undermines the correctness of the update for general φ.

comments.title: Mismatch between algorithm implementation and theory for KL divergence
Inconsistent handling of α → −1 limit for reverse-KL in Remark 2
comments.quote: When α = −1 (the reverse-KL divergence), the corresponding update scheme is πs[a][←]  1 π−s[a] η[−][η][|][A][|][(] [r] s[a][−][[(] [I][−][γP][ a]_[)] [v][π]_[]] [s]_[)] [/τ][+] [ c][s]  −1
comments.explanation: The update for α = −1 is given as π_{s,a} ← [ (1 - η)/π_{s,a} - η |A| ( (r - (I - γP^a)v_π)_s + c_s ) / τ ]^{-1}. First, this requires clarification of the limit. From (12), φ(x) = 1 - 4α²(1 - x^{(1+α)/2}) for α < 1. As α → -1, (1+α)/2 → 0, and x^{(1+α)/2} → 1 (since x^0 = 1), so φ(x) → 1 - 4(1)(1 - 1) = 1, a constant, which is problematic. Earlier, the paper states 'when α → -1 we obtain the reverse-KL divergence', but φ(x) = log(1/x) or x log x for KL variants. The definition φ(x) = 1 - 4α²(1 - x^{(1+α)/2}) does not converge to a reverse-KL function as α → -1; instead, it's more typical that α-divergence refers to D_α(p||q) = 4/(1-α²)(1 - ∫ q^{(1-α)/2} p^{(1+α)/2}), which for α → -1 gives reverse-KL. But the paper defines h_π via φ(|A| π_{s,a}), which suggests a pointwise f-divergence with generator φ. For reverse-KL, φ(x) = -log x or x log x depending on direction. The stated form φ(x) = 1 - 4α²(1 - x^{(1+α)/2}) with α → -1 doesn't yield this. So the claim in the text is questionable. Assuming it's a conventional α-divergence, the update rule in (13) uses σ = 2/(α-1). For α → -1, σ = 2/(-2) = -1. So the update π_{s,a} ← (c_s + x_a)^σ = (c_s + x_a)^{-1}. From line 13, x_a = (1-η)(π_{s,a})^{2/(1-α)} - [2α]η|A|/τ (r_s[a] - [...]_s), with exponent 2/(1-α). For α = -1, 2/(1 - (-1)) = 2/2 = 1, so x_a = (1-η) π_{s,a} - [2(-1)] η |A| /τ (...) = (1-η) π_{s,a} + 2 η |A| (...)/τ. But the quoted text has (1-η)/π_{s,a} instead of (1-η) π_{s,a}, suggesting a reciprocal error. This is likely an OCR error: 'π−s[a]' should be 'π_s[a]', and the exponent on π_{s,a} in x_a should be positive 1 (not -1). The final update is (c_s + x_a)^{-1}, which for α=-1 could correspond to a form of mirror descent for reverse-KL, but only if x_a were derived correctly. Given the discrepancy in x_a's dependence on π_{s,a}, the current OCR rendering suggests an error, but plausible correction resolves it. However, the limit α → -1 for this φ is mathematically dubious, making the claim weak.

comments.title: Misleading claim of quadratic convergence without qualification
comments.quote: We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'quadratic convergence', but Theorem 5, which provides the convergence result, requires several strong assumptions: (1) η = 1 (unit step size), (2) ∇f is Lipschitz near the solution, (3) θ^(k) → θ^*, and (4) the iterate must be sufficiently close to the optimum for quadratic convergence to manifest. Moreover, the result is local — it holds 'near the optimal policy', not globally. However, in the abstract and introduction, these qualifications are not mentioned. The numerical claim that 'the quasi-Newton method converges in single-digit iterations' might be true in experiments, but without stating that this likely depends on initialization and problem structure, it risks overstatement. In optimization, quadratic convergence is extremely fast but only in a local neighborhood. The paper does deliver on this in Theorem 5, so the claim isn't false, but presenting it upfront without the caveats could mislead readers into expecting global quadratic convergence or fast convergence from arbitrary initialization. The authors correctly prove a local result, but the high-level description overemphasizes the strength of the result. This is a common issue in ML papers, but still qualifies as a questionable claim.

comments.title: Underspecified multiplier computation for general φ
comments.quote: The determination of cs can be done in a similar way as in Ying (2020) based on the following lemma. The proof of this lemma can be found in Appendix C.
comments.explanation: Lemma 3 is stated for σ < 0, and Proposition 4 asserts that cs can be uniquely determined such that the updated policy is valid. However, the method assumes access to σ, which depends on α (σ = 2/(α-1)), but for general φ introduced in (12), σ must be derived from φ. In Algorithm 1, line 12 sets σ = 2/(α−1), implying α is given. But for general entropies beyond α-divergence, σ is not defined. The paper claims broad applicability to 'general convex functions', but the algorithm and multiplier computation are only detailed for the α-divergence case. Remark 3 mentions different priors, but not different φ. Thus, while Theorem 2 refers to 'general entropies', the method for finding c_s is only described for the α-family. A reader wishing to apply the method to, say, Tsallis entropy or another f-divergence, would not know how to compute c_s. The bisection method relies on the specific structure of the update π_new ∝ (c_s + x_a)^σ, which assumes a power-law mirror map. For arbitrary φ, the relation between π and θ = φ′(|A|π) is not invertible in closed form, and the constraint ∑_a π_{s,a} = 1 becomes a nonlinear equation in c_s that may not even be univariate monotonic. So while Lemma 3 applies to σ < 0, it doesn't extend automatically to arbitrary φ. This makes the method underspecified for the general case it claims to cover.

comments.title: Incorrect expression for α-divergence in terms of φ
comments.explanation: The passage attempts to define the α-divergence via a convex function φ such that (hπ)_s = (1/|A|) ∑_a φ(|A|π_s[a]). It states that when φ(x) = 1 − (4 α² / (1−α²)) (1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) (as interpreted from the OCR noise), this corresponds to the α-divergence. However, standard definitions of α-divergence (e.g., in Amari's information geometry) are of the form D_α(p||q) ∝ ∑ [ (p_i^{(1+α)/2} − q_i^{(1+α)/2})^2 ] for α ≠ ±1, which differs significantly from the expression given. In particular, the α-divergence is typically derived from φ(u) = (4/(1−α²)) (1 − u^{(1−α)/2} − (1−u)u^{(1−α)/2}) or similar, not matching the one provided. The derivation from φ to the final expression in (12) does not align with standard f-divergence construction where h_φ(π||μ) = ∑ μ_a φ(π_a / μ_a). With uniform prior μ_a = 1/|A|, this would require φ(|A|π_s[a]) scaled appropriately, but the expression given yields a different functional form. While the authors' intent is to define a general φ-based entropy, the specific choice for α-divergence appears mathematically incorrect or at least non-standard, which affects the validity of Theorem 2 and Algorithm 1 when α-divergence is used.

comments.title: Hessian diagonal approximation uses undefined 'wππs[a]' notation
comments.quote: where H[˜] is a diagonal matrix given by H[˜] ( sa ) , ( tb ) = −τδ{ ( sa ) , ( tb ) } ( wππs[a] ) s
comments.explanation: The expression (wππs[a])_s is ambiguous. From context, wπ := Zπ^{−⊤} e = (I − γPπ)^{−⊤} e, a vector in ℝ^{|S|}, and πs[a] is a scalar (probability). The term (wππs[a])_s suggests a state-indexed quantity, but the juxtaposition 'wππs[a]' is not defined. It likely means wπ[s] ⋅ πs[a], i.e., the product of the s-th component of wπ and the probability πs[a]. This interpretation is supported by the connection to natural policy gradient, where the Fisher metric involves πs[a] and a value-like term. However, the notation is non-standard and could be misread as a matrix-vector product or tensor. Given that this defines the core preconditioner of the proposed method, the lack of clarity in a key mathematical object is problematic. The OCR could have corrupted spacing (e.g., wπ[s] πs[a]), but even assuming that, the absence of explicit subscripting makes this error-prone.

comments.title: Sign error in quasi-Newton update for Shannon entropy
comments.explanation: The update rule for θs[a] = log πs[a] is stated as θs[a] ← η [(r_s[a] − τ − [(I−γP^a)v_π]_s + c_s)/τ] + (1−η)θs[a]. However, from the regularized Bellman equation v = rπ − τhπ + γPπ v, and the gradient of the objective e^⊤ vπ, the natural gradient direction for Shannon entropy should depend on the advantage-like term r_s[a] − [(I−γP^a)v_π]_s, but the subtraction of τ is suspicious. The entropy term hπ contributes −τ ∂hπ/∂π_s[a] = −τ (1 + log πs[a]) to the gradient. In the natural gradient update with Fisher metric ∼ diag(πs[a]), this leads to an update involving (gradient) / πs[a] ∼ (advantage − τ(1 + log πs[a])) / 1, and when parameterized by θs[a] = log πs[a], the flow becomes dθs[a]/dt ∝ advantage − τ(1 + θs[a]). Discretizing this would give θs[a] ← θs[a] + η (advantage_s[a] − τ) / τ + ... if preconditioned by 1/τ diag(πs[a]), but the exact form depends on the derivation in Appendix A. The presence of −τ in the numerator without clear justification suggests a potential sign or constant error. Given that Remark 1 claims equivalence to Cen et al. (2020), which uses a different constant handling, this may be resolvable by context. Still, as written, it appears to subtract τ from the reward, which lacks immediate justification.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the meaning of 'O(∥π − π*∥)' for matrix remainder
comments.quote: Hr is a remainder that vanishes at π = π[∗], i.e., Hr = O(∥π − π[∗]∥)
comments.explanation: The statement claims Hr = O(∥π − π*∥), where Hr is a matrix (the remainder of the Hessian). However, big-O notation for matrix-valued functions requires specification of the norm. The claim would make sense if it meant ∥Hr∥ = O(∥π − π*∥) for some matrix norm, implying the remainder tends to zero as π → π*. This is plausible, as at the optimum, the Hessian of the constraint and the objective may align such that off-diagonal curvature disappears. However, without specifying the norm or clarifying that it is the operator norm or Frobenius norm, the statement is ambiguous. In numerical analysis, O(∥π−π*∥) for a matrix could be misinterpreted as element-wise or in spectral norm. While experts might assume the standard interpretation, for a theoretical convergence proof (e.g., Theorem 5), the matrix norm behavior is critical. This ambiguity could mislead a careful reader about the strength of the approximation.

comments.title: Missing specification of cs in update rule for general φ
comments.explanation: The general update for θs[a] = φ′(|A|πs[a]) uses the same form as the Shannon entropy case, including the Lagrange multiplier cs, but does not specify how cs is determined for general φ. In the Shannon case, cs can be solved to enforce the simplex constraint because the update has a log-link form. However, for general φ, the relationship between θ and π is different (e.g., for Hellinger, θ ∝ √π), and the constraint ∑_a πs[a] = 1 may require a different nonlinear solver for cs. While Proposition 4 later states that cs can be determined, this is introduced only after Theorem 2, meaning the reader encounters the update rule without knowing how cs is computed. This creates a gap in reproducibility for general entropies. The algorithmic implementation in Algorithm 1 uses a bisection method for α-divergence, but this is not referenced in Theorem 2's statement. The underspecification weakens the presentation of the general method.

comments.title: Inconsistent entropy definition between Theorem 1 and general form
comments.quote: Let hπ ∈ ℝ^{|S|} be the negative Shannon entropy ( hπ ) s =[] a[π] s[a] [log] [ π] s[a]. When φ(x) = x log x, (hπ)s = ∑_a π_s[a] log π_s[a] − log |A| 1
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 defines (hπ)_s as ∑_a πs[a] log πs[a] (negative Shannon entropy), but in the general f-divergence discussion, it states that φ(x) = x log x leads to (hπ)_s = ∑_a πs[a] log πs[a] − log |A|, implying an additional constant. However, it then says 'by omitting the constant log |A| we recover the regularization used in Theorem 1'. This is inconsistent: either the constant is included in hπ or not. In optimization, additive constants do not affect gradients, so this may seem benign. But in the Hessian calculation (which involves second derivatives of hπ), constants vanish anyway. The real issue is that the definition of hπ shifts between being raw entropy and entropy up to constants. While technically harmless for gradients, it creates confusion in the formal definition. The paper should consistently define hπ with or without constants, especially since regularization strength τ scales the term directly.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameterization in Theorem 2 and Remark 2 for Hellinger and reverse-KL updates
comments.quote: _When α_ = 0 _(the Hellinger divergence), the corresponding update scheme is πs[a][←]_  ~~_√_~~ 1 _−πηs[a][−][η]_  _|A|_ ( _rs[a][−]_[[(] _[I][−][γP][ a]_[)] _[v][π]_[]] _[s]_[)] _[/τ]_[+] _[ c][s]_  _−_ 2 _, which can be obtained by inserting α_ = 0 _in (13). When α_ = _−_ 1 _(the reverse-KL divergence), the corresponding update scheme is πs[a][←]_  1 _π−s[a] η[−][η][|][A][|]_[(] _[r] s[a][−]_[[(] _[I][−][γP][ a]_[)] _[v][π]_[]] _[s]_[)] _[/τ]_[+] _[ c][s]_  _−_ 1 _, which can be obtained by inserting α_ = _−_ 1 _._
comments.explanation: At first glance, the update expressions in Remark 2 appear to be derived from (13) by substituting α = 0 and α = −1 into the α-divergence framework. However, checking the definition of φ in Theorem 2, φ(x) = 1 − (4α²)^{-1}(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) for α < 1. When α → 0, this converges to (1 − x^{1/2}), consistent with Hellinger (up to constants). The parameterization is θ_{s,a} = φ'(|A|π_{s,a}) ∝ (π_{s,a})^{-1/2}, so the update in (14) should be on θ-space. But Remark 2 gives direct updates on π_{s,a}, claiming they are obtained by inserting α = 0 or α = −1 into (13), which updates π via θ ← η(...) + (1−η)θ. So unless (13) is meant to define π-update from θ-update via inversion, the quoted expressions miss the inversion step: e.g., for Hellinger, we should have θ_{s,a} updated, then π_{s,a} ∝ θ_{s,a}^{-2}. The proposed update (1−η √π_{s,a} − ...)^{-2} does not match standard inversion. Similarly for reverse-KL (α→−1), φ'(x) ∝ 1/x implies θ_{s,a} ∝ 1/π_{s,a}, so inversion gives π_{s,a} ∝ 1/θ_{s,a}, but the quoted expression gives (1−η π_{s,a} − ...)^{-1}, which is inconsistent. The issue is that the 'obtained by inserting' suggests direct substitution into (13), but (13) is defined via parameterized θ-updates, while Remark 2 writes π-updates. Either (13) should be clarified as the definition of the θ-update, or the inversion must be made explicit. As written, a reader might incorrectly implement the update. However, given that Algorithm 1 uses inversion via exponentiation (for KL) or power (for α-divergence), the implementation seems correct — it's only the textual claim in Remark 2 that is misleading.

comments.title: Incorrect formula in update step for α-divergence in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The expression for xa in line 13 of Algorithm 1 contains several OCR-like artifacts: '(πs[a]_)] 2 _−_[1]' likely means (π_{s,a})^{(1−α)/2} or similar, since earlier φ' is tied to α via exponent (1+α)/2. More seriously, the term '2αη|A| 2' suggests a missing operator and possibly a wrong coefficient. Looking at the theoretical update (13): π_{s,a}^+ should be computed via θ_{s,a} = φ'(|A|π_{s,a}) and the θ-update in (14). For α-divergence, φ'(x) = x^{(α−1)/2} (up to scaling), so θ ∝ (π_{s,a})^{(α−1)/2}. Then (14) gives θ_{s,a}^+ = η[(r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v^π]_s + c_s)/τ] + (1−η)θ_{s,a}. To get π_{s,a}^+, we invert: π_{s,a}^+ ∝ (θ_{s,a}^+)^{2/(α−1)}. In Algorithm 1, line 15 does (c_s + x_a)^σ with σ = 2/(α−1), which is correct. But line 13 sets x_a = (1−η)(π_{s,a})^{something} − [2αη|A|/2] (r − ...)/τ. The first term should be (1−η) times the old θ, i.e., (1−η) (|A| π_{s,a})^{(α−1)/2}. But (πs[a])_)] 2 _−_[1] suggests (π_{s,a})^{(1−α)/2} (since (1−α)/2 = −(α−1)/2), so sign is flipped. Worse, the coefficient involving α and |A| is unclear: '2αη|A| 2' likely meant (2η|A| α something), but proper coefficient from θ-update is η (r − ...)/τ times derivative scaling. The term should be − [η (r − ...)/τ] / [φ'' or something]? Actually, from (14), θ^+ = η[...] + (1−η)θ, so the 'increment' in θ is η[...] − ηθ, so x_a should be η(1−η)? No — Algorithm 1 estimates c_s via bisection such that sum over a of (c_s + x_a)^σ = 1. From the structure, x_a should be linear in the gradient term. The correct form should derive from: θ_{s,a}^+ = (1−η)θ_{s,a} + η f_s,a, so (c_s + x_a)^σ = (π_{s,a}^+) ∝ (θ_{s,a}^+)^σ ∝ ( (1−η)θ_{s,a} + η [...] )^σ. But θ_{s,a} ∝ (π_{s,a})^{(α−1)/2} = (π_{s,a})^{- |σ|^{-1} since σ=2/(α−1). So (1−η) (π_{s,a})^{(α−1)/2} should appear, but in code it's (1−η)(π_{s,a})^{(1−α)/2} which is (1−η)/(π_{s,a})^{|(1+α)/2|}? This is inconsistent. Moreover, the gradient coefficient is missing a factor. Given that in the Hellinger limit (α=0, σ=−2), θ ∝ π^{-1/2}, so θ^+ ∝ (1−η)/√π + η(...)/τ, then π^+ ∝ (c + x)^(-2), so x should involve (1−η)/√π and η(...)/τ. But the equation in line 13 multiplies the gradient term by α and |A|—why? There's no justification. This suggests a real error in the algorithm implementation, potentially due to miscalculating the derivative or normalization. OCR damage makes it ambiguous, but even with correction (e.g., '2αη|A| 2' → 'η |A| (1−α)/2'), it does not align. This could break the algorithm numerically.

comments.title: Ambiguous derivation of c_s in update schemes
comments.quote: The remaining problem in the update schemes above is the determination of the multipliers _cs_ , since they cannot be solved explicitly as in the case of the negative Shannon entropy (Theorem 1).
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that for general φ, c_s (state-wise Lagrange multipliers for simplex constraint) cannot be solved in closed form, unlike Shannon entropy where exponential normalization suffices. They cite Lemma 3 and use bisection in Algorithm 1. However, the connection between Lemma 3 and the update scheme is underspecified. Lemma 3 solves ∑_i (x + x_i)^σ = 1 for x given x_i, σ < 0, which matches the form in Algorithm 1: sum_a (c_s + x_a)^σ = 1. But the derivation of x_a from the gradient term is missing in the main text — it relies entirely on Algorithm 1's line 13. As discussed, that line is corrupted. So while the use of bisection is sound in principle, the exact functional form of x_a (which depends on η, τ, gradient, and π) is not clearly derived from the quasi-Newton update. A reader would have to reverse-engineer it from the flawed line 13. The full derivation should be in Appendix B or D, but is not confirmed here. Given the critical dependence on correct normalization for stability and convergence, this gap is concerning, especially as implementation errors could go unnoticed.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated φ for α-divergence and reverse-KL limit
comments.explanation: The given φ(x) = 1 − (4α²)^{-1}(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) for α < 1. In the text, it states that reverse-KL is obtained as α → −1. But φ'(x) ∝ x^{(α−1)/2}, so as α → −1, φ'(x) ∝ x^{-1}, so θ ∝ 1/π, leading to log parameterization — correct for reverse-KL. But φ(x) itself as α → −1: x^{(1+α)/2} → x^0 = 1, so 1−x^0=0, and α²→1, so φ(x)→0 — this vanishes. Actually, the standard α-divergence is usually defined as (4/(1−α²)) (1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) so that limit α→−1 gives reverse-KL. Here, the coefficient is (4α²)^{-1} instead of 4/(1−α²), which does not converge properly. For α near −1, 4α² ≈ 4, so coefficient ≈ 1/4, while 4/(1−α²) blows up since α²→1. So the normalization is incorrect, and φ(x) does not converge to the reverse-KL divergence (which is −log π). The expression in (12) has |A|(1−α²), which may compensate, but (12) defines (hπ)_s, not φ. In (12): (hπ)_s = |A|(1−α²) ∑_a [1 − (|A|π_{s,a})^{(1+α)/2}], so with φ(x) = 1−x^{(1+α)/2}, hπ_s = |A|(1−α²) ∑φ(|A|π_{s,a}). So φ itself is not scaled. But in the text, φ(x) is written with 1−(4α²)^{-1}(1−x^{(1+α)/2}), which introduces an extra factor. This suggests an OCR error or transcription error: likely the authors meant φ(x) = 1−x^{(1+α)/2}, and the (4α²)^{-1} is spurious. Otherwise, φ is not convex (for α≠0), and limits fail. Given that in the reverse-KL limit they claim correct recovery, the likely intended φ is φ(x) = 1−x^{(1+α)/2}, so the quoted formula has an OCR-inserted factor. But without access to clean original, it's possible authors miscalculated normalization. Still, the issue affects theoretical claims about recovery of classical divergences.

comments.title: Unjustified reuse of wπ in mirror descent connection
comments.quote: where diag( _wπ_ ( _k_ )) is the diagonal matrix with the diagonal equal to _wπ_ ( _k_ ) := ( _I − γPπ[⊤]_[(] _[k]_[)][)] _[−]_[1] _[e]_[,]
comments.explanation: In the mirror descent connection, the authors introduce diag(wπ(k)) ⊗ I_{|A|} as a preconditioner, identifying β_s = 1/(wπ(k))_s as adaptive learning rates. However, wπ was originally defined in Section 2 as Zπ^{-⊤} e, where Zπ = I − γPπ. Crucially, this wπ appears in the gradient ∇E(π) = rπ + γPπ vπ − τ Dhπ − wπ c. In the mirror descent derivation leading to (20), the term diag(wπ(k)) appears as part of the Bregman divergence scaling. But in the standard mirror descent with entropy regularizer, the preconditioner comes from the Hessian of the regularizer (e.g., Fisher for KL), not from wπ, which is a value-like dual variable. The wπ here is a vector of 'dual features' or 'visit counts'. Its appearance in the mirror descent analogy seems post-hoc: the authors match the update form and 'suggest' an interpretation. But there's no derivation that the mirror descent with this particular Bregman divergence would yield this exact preconditioner. Moreover, in regular mirror descent, the geometry comes from the regularizer Φ, not from the dynamics via wπ. So while the algebraic manipulation may hold, the semantic claim that this is an 'accelerated mirror descent with adaptive learning rates β_s = 1/(wπ)_s' is unjustified. It could be a useful analogy, but as written, it risks misattribution of mechanism. The connection is plausible but requires more careful derivation to support the strong claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of norm in quadratic convergence plots
comments.quote: we draw the plots of log | log ∥π − π∗ ∥F | in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where π∗ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm.
comments.explanation: At first glance, plotting log |log ∥π − π∗∥F| seems odd because for small errors, ∥π − π∗∥F < 1, so log ∥π − π∗∥F < 0, and taking absolute value then log again yields log |log ∥π − π∗∥F| = log(−log ∥π − π∗∥F), which is standard for visualizing quadratic convergence. This transformation maps quadratic convergence ∥e_{k+1}∥ ≤ C∥e_k∥² to a straight line with slope log 2 in the log |log ∥e∥| plot — a known trick in numerical analysis. The issue arises in the notation: the text says 'log | log ∥π − π∗ ∥F |', with absolute value bars, but if ∥π − π∗∥F < 1, then log ∥π − π∗∥F is negative, so |log ∥π − π∗∥F| = −log ∥π − π∗∥F, and thus log |log ∥π − π∗∥F| = log(−log ∥π − π∗∥F). This is correct and commonly used. However, the notation 'log | log ∥π − π∗ ∥F |' is ambiguous: the absolute value could be misinterpreted as being around the entire log expression rather than just the inner log. But given that the authors then say 'shall be parallel to the reference line' with slope log 2, and this matches standard asymptotic convergence diagnostics, the intent is clear. Moreover, Figure captions consistently describe it as 'convergence of log | log ∥π − π∗ ∥F |'. Thus, while slightly notationally awkward, it is not erroneous. No real issue remains.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of f(π) in Theorem 5
comments.quote: Let η = 1 , f ( π ) sa = − ( rs[a]−[((I−γP a)] vπ ]) s )  and
comments.explanation: The expression for f(π)_{sa} is given as −(r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s). From context, [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s is (v_π)_s − γ∑_t P_{st}[a] (v_π)_t, which is the standard Bellman evaluation for action a. Then r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s is exactly the advantage-like term, and negating it gives −(r_{s,a} − (v_π)_s + γ∑_t P_{st}[a] (v_π)_t). In the update rules (e.g., (13)), we see terms like r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s appearing directly in the exponent or power, without a sign flip. However, in Theorem 5, f(π)_{sa} is defined with a negative sign. This could initially seem inconsistent, especially since in the mirror descent derivation (equation (20)), f(π[k]) appears in ∇Φ(π[k+1]) − ∇Φ(π[k]) = −β (... + f(π[k]) + ...). But in the Algorithm 1 α-divergence update, the term (1−η)(π_{s,a})^{(1−α)/2} − (η|A|/τ)(r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s) is used, which for η=1 becomes −(|A|/τ)(r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s). Meanwhile, under log parameterization, ∇Φ(π) corresponds to φ′(|A|π_{s,a}), and the update in θ-space is meant to move in the direction of the gradient. Since the objective is maximization of E(π), the gradient should be positive in the direction of improvement. However, in the mirror descent form (20), the update subtracts a positive multiple of f(π[k]) if f(π[k]) is aligned with the gradient direction. This only makes sense if f(π[k]) is defined as the negative gradient, i.e., f = −∇E. Indeed, in Section 2, E(π) is defined with e^⊤ v_π, and the gradient ∇E(π) is related to Q-function terms, which are r + γPv. So −(r − (I−γP_a)v) = −r + (I−γP_a)v is not obviously the gradient. But actually, from Eq. (7), ∇E(π) involves Q_{s,a} − V_s + τ∂h/∂π, and (I−γP_a)v_s = v_s − γ∑_t P_{st}[a]v_t. The term r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s = r_{s,a} − v_s + γ∑_t P_{st}[a]v_t = (r_{s,a} + γ∑_t P_{st}[a]v_t) − v_s = Q_{s,a} − V_s, which is the advantage. So f(π)_{sa} = −(Q_{s,a} − V_s) = −A_{s,a}. Then in the mirror descent update, we subtract a term proportional to f(π) = −A, which is equivalent to adding a positive multiple of the advantage — correct for maximization. Therefore, despite initial confusion, the negative sign in f(π) is correct: it makes f = −∇_π E(π) up to constants and constraints. The context resolves this — the definition is consistent with the mirror descent interpretation. No error.

comments.title: α = −3 violates assumption α < 1 but σ < 0 required in Lemma 3
comments.quote: we implement Algorithm 1 with the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the α-divergence with α = −3.
comments.explanation: Lemma 3, which guarantees existence and uniqueness of the multiplier c_s via bisection, assumes σ < 0. Since σ := 2/(α−1), for α = −3, we have σ = 2/(−3−1) = 2/(−4) = −0.5 < 0, so σ < 0 holds. The assumption α < 1 is stated in the 'Key Claims' section, and α = −3 satisfies α < 1. Although α = −3 is far from typical (e.g., α = 0,−1), the derivation for α-divergence in Theorem 2 and the update (13) are defined for α < 1, and the bisection method via Lemma 3 applies as long as σ < 0. The only potential concern is whether the α-divergence is well-defined for α = −3. From the definition in Eq. (12), φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}), with α < 1. For α = −3, (1+α)/2 = (−2)/2 = −1, so φ(x) involves x^{−1}, i.e., 1/x. Then (hπ)_s ∝ ∑_a (1 − |A|π_{s,a}^{−1}), which blows up as π_{s,a} → 0+, but is finite for π_{s,a} > 0. The Bregman potential Φ would then involve integrating φ′(x) ∝ x^{−2}, so Φ(π) ∝ ∑_{s,a} π_{s,a}^{−1}, which is convex for π_{s,a} > 0. So the method is mathematically valid, though numerically sensitive near boundary. The paper assumes π_{s,a} > 0 in the domain for Lipschitzness of ∇f, so α = −3 is acceptable. Authors are justified in using it, though they may want to note numerical care is needed. Not an error.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in Algorithm 1 line 8 for KL divergence
comments.explanation: Line 8 appears corrupted due to OCR. The intended expression is likely the standard KL-regularized NPG update. From Remark 1, when KL divergence is used, the update should match entropy-regularized NPG. In Theorem 1, the update is θ_{s,a} ← η(r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s + c_s)/τ + (1−η)θ_{s,a}. For η=1 and θ_{s,a} = log π_{s,a}, this gives log π_{new,s,a} = (r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s + c_s)/τ. Exponentiating, π_{new,s,a} ∝ exp((r_{s,a} − [(I−γP_a)v_π]_s)/τ). The 'c_s' handles normalization. The OCR output shows 'ex η[exp(] η[(] η[ r][(] s[a][r] ...' which seems like it should be 'exp( (r_{s,a} + ...)/τ )' — likely 'ex' is 'exp', and 'η[ r][(] s[a][r]' is 'r_{s,a}'. The double 'η' and repetition 'r][(] s[a][r' suggest OCR duplication. The structure 'exp( η ( r_{s,a} + γP_a v_π )_s / τ )' would make sense for η=1. But the text says 'η[ r][(] s[a][r] [+] s[a][(][+(] γ[ P][γP]', suggesting 'r_{s,a} + (γP_a v_π)_s', but this would be Q_{s,a}, while the correct term is r_{s,a} − (I−γP_a)v_π)_s = (r_{s,a} + γP_a v_π)_s − v_s. The v_s term is missing. However, in the natural gradient update, the state-dependent baseline (v_s) can be absorbed into the Lagrange multiplier c_s because it is independent of a. So exp((r_{s,a} + γP_a v_π)_s / τ + c'_s) is equivalent to including −v_s in c_s. So the absence of −v_s is not an error — it's incorporated into c_s. The normalization via c_s in the KL case is explicit (log-sum-exp), while for α-divergence it's via Lemma 3. The OCR corruption makes the exact formula unreadable, but the intent is clear from context (Theorem 1), and the method works. Since it's likely OCR, and the correct form can be inferred, we do not flag this as an author error.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated convergence threshold and implied precision in Experiment II
comments.quote: the convergence threshold as ϵ = 10^{−12} and the learning rate η as 1. All the tests end up with fast convergence as shown in Figure 2(a)...
comments.explanation: The paper states the convergence threshold is ϵ = 10^{−12}, defined in Algorithm 1 as q = ∥π_new − π∥_F / ∥π∥_F < ϵ_tol. In Figure 2(a), the y-axis shows ∥π_new − π∥_F / ∥π∥_F on log scale, and the curves drop to around 10^{-12}, consistent with the threshold. Table 1 reports only the number of iterations and BiCGSTAB steps, not the final error. No contradiction arises. The text says 'converge in 6, 6, 6, 5 iterations', which matches the drop below 10^{-12} in the plots. The threshold is stringent and achieved. No issue.

comments.title: Ambiguous Interpretation of α-Divergence Parameter in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: the α-divergence with α = −3
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the α-divergence with the functional form φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}), defined for α < 1. In Section 4.1, it states that experiments were run with α = −3. While this value is within the defined range (α < 1), it raises concern because the typical family of α-divergences is often parameterized such that α ∈ [−1,1] or symmetric around 0 (e.g., KL at α=1, reverse-KL at α=−1, Hellinger at α=0). An α = −3 is unusually far outside the conventional range. Initially, I suspected an OCR or sign error (e.g., perhaps α = −0.3 or α = 0.3), but the text consistently refers to α = −3 in multiple places (e.g., in claims about log|log‖π−π*‖F| plots across α=−3), suggesting it is intentional. The issue is not mathematical impossibility—φ is still convex for x>0 when α=−3—but the practical and interpretative implications are unclear. The function φ(x) = 1 − 4(9)(1 − x^{−1}) = 1 − 36(1 − 1/x) = 36/x − 35, which leads to hπ being related to the negative first moment of 1/πs[a], a highly unstable and potentially poorly behaved regularization. While not technically incorrect, using such an extreme α without justification (e.g., why this improves convergence or robustness) risks being misleading or numerically dubious. The authors demonstrate convergence in 6 iterations (fewer than for α=0 or α=−1), which is counterintuitive unless the curvature is unusually favorable. This parameter choice is underspecified: no discussion is given of how φ′ is computed for α=−3 or how the bisection in Algorithm 1 remains stable when updating (cs + xa)^σ with σ = 2/(α−1) = 2/(−4) = −0.5, leading to inverse square roots that could amplify numerical errors. Thus, while not mathematically invalid, the lack of justification and potential numerical fragility makes this a moderate concern.

comments.title: Incorrect Update Rule for KL Divergence in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: (π_new)_s^a ← (π_s^a)^{(1−η)} exp(η(η r_s^a + (γP^a v_π)_s)/τ) )
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 8 presents the update rule for KL divergence. The expression contains a redundant and incorrect double use of η: 'exp(η(η r_s^a + ...))' implies η² r_s^a, which is wrong. From Theorem 1 and standard entropy-regularized NPG updates, the correct update should be π_s^a ∝ π_s^a^{1−η} exp(η (r_s^a − (I−γP^a)v_π)_s / τ). Here, the term (r_s^a − [(I−γP^a)v_π]_s) is the advantage-like quantity. But in the algorithm, it is written as 'η r_s^a + (γP^a v_π)_s', which has a sign error (should be minus, not plus) and incorrect scaling (r_s^a multiplied by η twice). Moreover, the Bellman term should be subtracted, not added: f(π)_sa = −(r_s^a − [(I−γP^a)v_π]_s) in Theorem 5. Thus, the update should involve exp( −η f(π)_s^a / τ ), which expands to exp( η (r_s^a − (I−γP^a)v_π)_s / τ ). The current expression has 'η(η r_s^a + (γP^a v_π)_s)', which incorrectly adds the value term and squares the learning rate on the reward. This appears to be more than an OCR error—it is structurally wrong. The correct update for KL with θ_s^a = log π_s^a and η=1 should be θ_new ← θ + (r − (I−γP)v_π)/τ + c_s, which exponentiates to π_new ∝ π exp((r − (I−γP)v_π)/τ). The given form does not match. This undermines reproducibility and correctness of the KL baseline used in experiments, a key comparison point. Given that KL is a main benchmark and this error would cause divergence in practice, this is a major issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of α in α-Divergence
comments.explanation: The paper defines φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) for α < 1, and states that α = 0 gives Hellinger and α → −1 gives reverse-KL. Let us verify: for α=0, φ(x) = 1 − 0 = 1, which is constant—clearly not Hellinger. This suggests a misdefinition. The standard α-divergence generator is φ(x) = 4/(1−α²) (1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) for α ≠ ±1, rescaled so that φ′′(1)=1. The factor 4α² does not match. For α → −1, φ(x) → 1 − 4(1)(1 − x^0) = 1 − 4(1−1) = 1, again constant. Only if we ignore the 4α² and consider φ(x) ∝ 1 − x^{(1+α)/2} does it align with standard f-divergences. For α=0: (1+α)/2 = 0.5, so φ(x) ∝ 1 − √x → related to Hellinger. For α→−1: (1+α)/2→0, so x^0=1, but limit is logarithmic. This suggests the 4α² is a normalization error. But the paper later uses φ′ in Algorithm 1, so the derivative matters. For their φ(x), φ′(x) = −4α² ⋅ (−(1+α)/2) x^{(α−1)/2} = 2α²(1+α) x^{(α−1)/2}. For α→−1, this → 0, not diverging as needed for reverse-KL (which requires log-like behavior). But Remark 2 claims α=−1 gives reverse-KL. Only if the generator were φ(x)=x log x or (x−1)−log x does it yield KL or reverse-KL. The standard correspondence is: α-divergence with parameter α has generator proportional to x^{(1+α)/2}. The factor 4α² appears misplaced. Either it's a typo (should be 4/(1−α²)) or α is reparameterized. But the update in Theorem 2 depends on φ′, so an incorrect φ leads to wrong update. This contradicts the claim that α→−1 gives reverse-KL. Context does not resolve this: the derivation in Theorem 2 assumes the given φ, so the entire update is based on a flawed generator. This affects all α-divergence results, including experiments. Even if OCR corrupted the expression, the form '1 − 4α²[(1 − x...]' is consistently used. This is a major technical flaw.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Assumed Convergence Criterion and Algorithm Output
comments.quote: q = ‖π_new − π‖_F / ‖π‖_F
comments.explanation: The paper states in the key definitions and in Algorithm 1 that the convergence threshold is ϵ = 10^−12, and convergence is determined by q > ϵ_tol. The stopping condition is q = ‖π_new − π‖_F / ‖π‖_F < ϵ_tol. However, this relative change in the policy is not equivalent to proximity to the optimal policy π*. Quadratic convergence is defined with respect to ‖π^(k) − π*‖ in Theorem 5. But Algorithm 1 monitors ‖π_new − π_old‖, which can be small even if far from π* (e.g., when trapped in a plateau). Conversely, near π*, ‖π_new − π*‖ may be small even if ‖π_new − π_old‖ is moderate due to curvature. For quadratic convergence to be empirically verified via log|log‖π−π*‖F| plots (as claimed), the stopping criterion must allow accurate measurement near π*. But if the algorithm stops based on ‖Δπ‖ < ϵ, it may terminate before reaching the quadratic phase, especially if ϵ=10^{-12} is based on ‖Δπ‖ rather than ‖π−π*‖. The authors claim to verify quadratic convergence down to machine precision, but cannot do so reliably if they do not track ‖π−π*‖. Moreover, ‖π‖_F for a probability vector is roughly √(|S||A|) ⋅ (1/|A|) = √(|S|/|A|), so ‖Δπ‖/‖π‖_F ≈ ‖Δπ‖ ⋅ √(|A|/|S|). For |S|=135k, |A|=2, this rescaling could delay termination unnecessarily. The use of relative policy change is practical but insufficient for validating theoretical convergence rates. The paper does not mention recomputing ‖π−π*‖ in experiments. This weakens the claim of empirical validation of quadratic convergence—a key contribution.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Bi-CGSTAB Solver Choice and Step Count
comments.quote: Total computational cost remains low: < 120 Bi-CGSTAB steps overall
comments.explanation: The paper claims the total number of Bi-CGSTAB steps is under 120 for the full algorithm across all iterations, which is central to the scalability claim. However, Bi-CGSTAB is an iterative solver used in Step 6 of Algorithm 1 to compute vπ = (I−γPπ)^{−1}(rπ − τhπ) in each iteration. Each outer quasi-Newton iteration requires one such solve. If the algorithm takes 7 iterations (as in Experiment I), and each requires multiple Bi-CGSTAB steps (inner iterations), then 120 total steps implies ~17 per outer iteration, which is plausible for a well-conditioned system. However, no information is given about the tolerance or convergence criterion for the Bi-CGSTAB solver itself. The overall policy convergence threshold is ϵ=10^{-12}, but it's unclear whether Bi-CGSTAB runs to the same precision. If it uses a loose tolerance, the value function inaccuracies could affect policy updates; if tight, the per-iteration cost may be higher. Moreover, Bi-CGSTAB for nonsymmetric systems can stagnate or require preconditioning, especially with γ close to 1 (as in experiments, γ=0.99), which worsens the condition number. The paper provides no evidence that Bi-CGSTAB converges in ~17 steps under these conditions. While Bi-CGSTAB is cited (reference not shown), its effectiveness on MDPs is non-standard—typically, value iteration or GMRES is used. Reference to de Pillis (1998) is general but not specific to MDPs. The claim of low total steps is critical to the scalability argument, but the lack of solver settings or convergence details makes it underspecified and hard to reproduce or trust.

comments.title: Undefined Parameter σ in Algorithm 1 for α-Divergence Case
comments.quote: Set σ = 2 / (α − 1)
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 12, σ is set as 2/(α−1). However, σ is not previously defined in the paper. Later, in Lemma 3 and Proposition 4, σ < 0 is assumed, and the equation Σ (x + xi)^σ = 1 is solved for x. For α=−3, σ=2/(−4)=−0.5, which satisfies σ<0. However, the paper never defines σ in terms of the entropy function. From Theorem 2, the update is π_new ∝ (cs + xa)^σ with σ = 2/(α−1). But this relationship is not derived or motivated. In f-divergence mirror descent, the mirror map is related to φ′, and updates are in dual space. Here, θ_s^a = φ′(|A|π_s^a), and the update is θ_new = (1−η)θ + η(rs[a] − [(I−γP^a)v]_s + cs)/τ. To recover π_new from θ_new, one needs the inverse of φ′. For the given φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1−x^{(1+α)/2}), φ′(x) = −4α² ⋅ (−(1+α)/2) x^{(α−1)/2} = 2α²(1+α) x^{(α−1)/2}. Solving θ = φ′(x) for x gives x ∝ θ^{2/(α−1)} = θ^σ. Thus σ = 2/(α−1) is indeed the exponent in the inverse mapping. While mathematically correct under the (flawed) φ), the issue is that σ is used in the algorithm without explanation or connection to φ. A reader cannot reproduce the method without knowing σ is the inverse of the mirror map exponent. This is a case of underspecification: the algorithm gives a 'magic number' σ without derivation or comment, even though it's critical to the procedure.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically compelling contribution by unifying entropy-regularized policy gradient methods within a quasi-Newton framework, offering a clear proof of local quadratic convergence that generalizes Natural Policy Gradient. The writing is generally lucid and well-structured, effectively motivating the limitations of current linear convergence rates and the potential of second-order methods. However, the scope of the work is significantly constrained by the assumption of finite state and action spaces, which may limit its immediate relevance to the broader deep RL community typically focused on continuous control or high-dimensional function approximation. Most significantly, the empirical claims made in the abstract and introduction—specifically that the proposed method converges in "single-digit iterations" on "industrial-scale" problems—are exceptionally aggressive and border on implausible; without a corresponding discussion of the computational overhead or per-iteration cost, these claims risk appearing hyperbolic and detracting from the paper's solid theoretical insights regarding the diagonal decomposition of the Hessian.

comments.title: Sign convention for entropy regularization
comments.quote: one often regularizes the problem with a function hπ such as the negative Shannon entropy (˜ hπ ) s =[] a[π] s[a][log] [ π] s[a]
comments.explanation: The text refers to this as 'negative Shannon entropy' but the formula Σ_a π_s[a] log π_s[a] is actually the (positive) entropy in RL conventions. In information theory, Shannon entropy is -Σ p log p. While this is likely just a convention difference (RL often defines entropy without the negative sign), the terminology 'negative Shannon entropy' is confusing. The authors should either use 'Shannon entropy' (following RL convention) or change the formula to -Σ_a π_s[a] log π_s[a] to match the information theory convention.

comments.title: Missing definition for e vector
comments.quote: we seek a policy π that maximizes e[⊤] vπ for some (in fact arbitrary) positive weight vector e ∈ R[|S|]
comments.explanation: The vector e is introduced here but not properly defined or motivated. While the authors claim it's arbitrary, in practice this vector represents the initial state distribution or importance weights over states. Without proper definition, readers might misunderstand the objective function. This should be clarified, especially since the choice of e affects the optimal policy.

comments.title: Inconsistent update forms in Theorem 2
comments.quote: the quasi-Newton method from \tilde{H}(\pi) (the policy gradient ascent preconditioned by -\tilde{H}(\pi)) is [\pi_s[a] \leftarrow (\pi_s[a]^{-\frac{1-\alpha}{2}} - \frac{\eta(1-\alpha^2)|A|}{2\tau}(r_s[a] - [(I-\gamma P^a)v_\pi]_s) + c_s)^{\frac{2}{1-\alpha}}] where 0 < \eta \leq 1 is the learning rate and with parameterization \theta_s[a] = \phi'(|A|\pi_s[a]), the update scheme above can be expressed as: \theta_s[a] \leftarrow \eta\left(\frac{r_s[a] - [(I-\gamma P^a)v_\pi]_s + c_s}{\tau}\right) + (1-\eta)\theta_s[a].
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 presents two different forms of the update rule without clearly explaining their relationship. The first updates π directly, while the second updates θ. The text claims the second 'can be expressed as' the first, but no derivation or explanation is provided for how these are equivalent. Given that θ_s[a] = φ'(|A|π_s[a]) where φ' is complex for α-divergence (φ'(x) = -(2/α)(1-x)^{(α-1)/2}), the relationship between these update forms is non-trivial and requires justification.

comments.explanation: The matrix B appears in the gradient equation without definition. While its role is clear from context (it must be the constraint matrix for ∑_a π_s[a] = 1), this should be explicitly defined for completeness, especially since this is a key equation in the derivation.

comments.explanation: Theorem 2 part (a) states the O(\|π - π*\|) property for the Hessian approximation, but this was previously claimed for the Shannon entropy case. The theorem should more clearly state that this property holds for the α-divergence case as well, rather than assuming the reader will infer this.
